title: Lilith and the First Refusal
dek: When your body says no before your mind catches up, it is not a failure. It is the ancient, somatic act of refusal—a boundary drawn in muscle and fascia.
bodyMarkdown:
## Lilith and the First Refusal: The Body Remembers

You know that moment. The one that isn’t marked on any calendar, but lives in the marrow of your bones and the sinew of your muscles. It’s the moment you first said no—not aloud, perhaps, but in the tightening of your belly, the clench of your jaw, the pulling back of your shoulder blades against some unseen weight. This is the first refusal, the primal boundary drawn in flesh before words existed to give it shape. It is a moment as old as stories of Lilith, the woman who refused to be anything less than herself, who would not bow her head or soften her edges to fit the demands of a world that wanted her smaller, quieter, contained.

When you breathe in this refusal now—maybe in the dark hours before dawn, when your psoas muscle coils like a trapped serpent around your lumbar spine—you can feel the lineage of that first no. It lives in the subtle tightening of your trapezius muscle, the one that bridges your neck and shoulder, where tension accumulates like sediment from years of holding back. And deep in your masseter, the jaw muscle that clenches when you are angry, scared, or defiant, the memory of that refusal lives on, unyielding and fierce.

## The Body’s Archive: Trauma and Memory in Flesh

Bessel van der Kolk teaches us that trauma is not just a story we tell ourselves; it is a physiological imprint. Trauma lodges itself in the fascia, the connective tissue that envelops every muscle, every organ, every cell. It lives in the way your diaphragm tightens, limiting your breath, or how the psoas muscle—your deepest core stabilizer—contracts and refuses to release, keeping you braced against a threat you may no longer consciously perceive.

This is not pathology. This is biology. The body remembers what the mind tries to forget. When you first refused—to say no to a demand, to an expectation, to a shape forced upon your being—it was a shock to the system. Your nervous system, wired to protect you, responded with a cascade of signals: your trapezius tensed to hold you upright and alert; your masseter clenched, ready to bite back; your psoas braced, holding your center safe.

The refusal is a wound and a weapon. It is the crack in the armor where your true self shines through. But it is also a place of vulnerability, where the body stores the cost of that defiance. You might notice this in the way your shoulders stiffen in the afternoon sun, or the subtle grinding of your teeth as you fall asleep, or the restless stirring in your belly in the quiet moments before dawn. These are not random aches or quirks of your anatomy. They are echoes of the first refusal.

## Lilith’s Legacy in Your Fascia

Lilith, in the stories that have outlived her, is often cast as a demon or an outcast. But she is also the first woman who refused to be less than whole, who said no to subjugation not with words, but with every fiber of her body. Her story is not one of failure or sin; it is one of embodied resistance.

When you feel the tight coil in your psoas or the relentless tension in your trapezius, you are in conversation with Lilith. You are feeling the pressure of that first refusal as it echoes through the centuries in your own body. The masseter, clenched in moments of silent fury, remembers her rage and her courage.

This is why somatic awareness matters. To hear the body’s messages—to feel the jaw’s clenched secret, the shoulder’s locked warning, the belly’s protective coil—is to honor Lilith’s legacy. She did not refuse with words alone. She refused with the language of muscle and fascia, with the blueprint of her nervous system.

## The First Refusal as a Somatic Event

Try this now: place your hand on your lower belly, just above the pubic bone. Feel the psoas muscle deep inside, hidden beneath layers of flesh. Can you sense that tightening, that contraction? It might be subtle, like a thread pulled taut beneath your skin. This is where your first refusal lives. It is the body’s original boundary, the place where you said no before you could say anything at all.

Look up to your trapezius. Notice the way it rises around your neck and shoulders, how it can stiffen under the weight of expectation or fear. That muscle holds your posture but also holds your history. When it tightens, it can feel like a burden pressing down, a reminder that to refuse is also to carry the consequences.

And finally, attend to your masseter. Run your fingers along your jawline. Can you feel the tension there—like a clenched fist held just beneath the skin? This muscle is the body’s fist, the first line of defense. It remembers every time you held back your voice, every time you bit your tongue, every time you refused to be silenced.

## The Unseen Cost of Saying No

The first refusal is a declaration of autonomy, but it is also the beginning of a complicated dialogue between your body and the world. When you say no, the body braces for impact. The psoas tightens, the trapezius rises, the masseter clenches. These are not just reactions; they are the body’s way of preparing you to stand firm, to survive the fallout.

Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that trauma is stored in the body because the body is the first responder. It cannot un-know what it has experienced. When you recall that first refusal, you might feel a tightening in your abdomen, a pull in your shoulders, a grinding in your jaw. These sensations are not signs of weakness or dysfunction; they are the body’s way of remembering, protecting, and preparing.

You are not alone in this. Every woman who was taught her fury was ugly carries this legacy in her muscles and fascia. But there is power in this knowledge. When you learn to listen to your body’s language, when you acknowledge the psoas’s coil, the trapezius’s rise, the masseter’s clench, you step into a lineage of resistance that stretches back to Lilith herself.

---

This first part of our journey into Lilith and the first refusal is a somatic invitation. It asks you to feel into the body’s memory, to honor the muscle’s story, and to recognize that your anger, your refusal, is not a failure but a survival strategy written in flesh. The body knows. The jaw remembers. This is not pathology, this is biology. And in that knowledge, there is a fierce, unyielding grace.

## The Anatomy of Refusal: Mapping Lilith in Your Body

It’s just past two in the morning, and the sky outside your window is a thick, slow-moving curtain of rain. The air smells of damp earth and cold stone, the kind of night that presses its weight into your skin. You lie awake, the sheets tangled around your hips, feeling the familiar coil in your psoas—a muscle that seems to hold not just your body but your history. Somewhere beneath the surface of your awareness, the story of Lilith stirs, and you realize her myth is not just a story to be told but a map etched into your anatomy.

The anatomy of refusal is not abstract. It is the body’s clear, physical language: the masseter muscle clenched tight enough to press teeth into gums, the trapezius pulled taut as if bracing against an invisible blow, the psoas coiled like a spring beneath the abdomen, holding you upright and ready to flee or fight. These muscles do not simply react; they remember. They are the living archive of every time you said no, every time you held your ground when the world asked for your shrinking.

Lilith’s refusal is embodied. She is not just a mythic figure cast out for defiance; she is a somatic blueprint for what it means to claim your boundaries through your body’s raw, unfiltered response. To feel Lilith in your muscle and fascia is to acknowledge that refusal is a complex, layered act—one that engages every fiber of you, from your breath to your bones.

## Lilith as a Somatic Map: The Body’s First No

Karla McLaren, whose work on anger as a messenger reshapes how we understand this fierce emotion, reminds us that anger is not the enemy. It is the body’s first responder, the signal flare that warns you when your boundaries are breached, your needs ignored, your autonomy threatened. Anger is the muscle memory of refusal.

When Lilith refused to bow to Adam’s will, she was embodying this primal anger in its purest form. She was the living, breathing embodiment of the body saying no before thought or language had the chance to soften the edges. Your masseter clenches in quiet fury when you remember that first no—the one that might have been met with silence, with scorn, or worse, with erasure. Your trapezius rises in defense, holding your head high even when the world demands that you look down. Your psoas locks your core, a visceral anchor in the storm of interpersonal conflict.

This refusal is lodged in the fascia that wraps around these muscles, a dense, fibrous web that records the tension, the contraction, the very act of boundary-setting. Your body does not forget. It remembers in the way your jaw tightens on cold mornings, in the shoulder tension that builds by mid-afternoon, in the low, persistent ache in your lower belly when you lie awake on nights thick with rain.

## The Night Speaks: Somatic Awareness in the Dark Hours

That night, the rain tapping a steady rhythm against your windowpane, you sense the familiar stirrings in your body. You place your hand just below your navel and feel the deep coil of the psoas muscle, contracting beneath your fingers like a secret kept tight. The trapezius hums with tension along your shoulder blades, as if bracing you for a confrontation you can’t yet name. Your masseter clenches involuntarily, the silent scream of anger held too long.

This is a somatic conversation with Lilith’s refusal. The body’s language is unspoken but clear: here is your power, here is your boundary, here is the anger that demands to be seen and heard. You breathe slowly into the tightness, acknowledging without judgment the physical story your muscles tell.

In these hours, when the world is hushed and the body’s whispers grow louder, you might reach for grounding medicines—herbs that honor the body’s nervous system and soothe the tension held so stubbornly in muscle and fascia. Organic Ashwagandha Root, for example, is a potent ally. Its adaptogenic properties help calm the nervous system without dulling your edge, allowing you to rest into the body’s wisdom while softening the grip of chronic tension. The earthiness of ashwagandha resonates with the dark soil of this rain-soaked night, inviting your psoas to unwind and your trapezius to lower its guard.

You can find [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) as a trusted companion in this somatic journey, a medicine that supports your body’s natural rhythm and respects the fierce intelligence of anger as a messenger.

## Anger as a Messenger: The Body’s First Responder

Anger often gets a bad reputation, especially for women who were raised to see their fury as ugly or dangerous. But Karla McLaren’s work insists that anger is a vital part of your emotional toolkit. It is neither villain nor victim; it is a first responder, arriving in your body before words can form. When you feel that clench in your masseter or the stiff climb of your trapezius, it is anger signaling that something matters deeply—your safety, your dignity, your right to be seen.

Lilith’s story gives you permission to claim that anger without shame. She is the embodiment of the refusal to be silenced, the refusal to dissolve into the roles imposed by others. Her myth is a somatic map, guiding you through the landscape of your own body’s response to injustice and boundary violation.

When your psoas tightens, it is not a failure of relaxation; it is your core saying, “This is mine. This is where I hold my power.” When your trapezius rises, it is your neck and shoulders preparing to carry the weight of your truth, even when it feels heavy. When your masseter clenches, it is your jaw remembering the taste of resistance, the grit of refusal.

## The Body’s Story Is Never a Lie

That night, as the rain softens to a drizzle, you allow yourself to feel the full spectrum of what refusal means in your body. There is tension, yes, but also strength. There is anger, yes, but also clarity. The muscles hold these stories not as scars but as living, breathing parts of your selfhood.

The body’s story is never a lie. It does not moralize or judge; it simply remembers. The masseter remembers your first clenched no. The trapezius remembers the burden of standing tall. The psoas remembers the deep coil of refusal that keeps your center safe.

In this way, Lilith’s myth is not an ancient tale to be feared or dismissed. It is a somatic map, a guide to understanding how your body holds and carries refusal. It is a call to listen—to the ache in your shoulders, the tightness in your belly, the grinding in your jaw—and to honor these sensations as vital messengers.

As dawn approaches, and the rain fades into a quiet mist, you settle into this somatic knowing. You breathe in, feeling your psoas soften just a little, your trapezius lower its guard, your masseter unclench. You carry Lilith’s refusal in your body, fierce and unyielding, a testament to the power of saying no when the world demands yes.

This is your birthright. This is your medicine. And the night, with its rain and silence, holds you in the fierce grace of refusal.

## Herbs for Refusal: Ashwagandha and Chamomile in Your Body’s Language

The body’s refusal—the clenched masseter, the coiled psoas, the tight trapezius—is a language written in muscle and fascia, breath and blood. It is raw, unfiltered, and often misunderstood. But there is medicine in the earth that speaks to this language, aligning with the body’s intelligence rather than working against it. Two herbs stand out as allies in the somatic journey of refusal: Ashwagandha and Chamomile.

Ashwagandha, with its deep, earthy root, is a tonic for the nervous system that doesn’t ask you to dull your edge or soften your boundaries. Instead, it strengthens the body’s resilience, helping the psoas untangle just enough to hold firm without rigid contraction. It is a fierce, steady herb that supports your body’s ability to carry the weight of refusal without breaking or shutting down. Chamomile, by contrast, soothes the sharp edges of tension that gather in the trapezius and calms the nervous system’s alarm bells. It is not a sedative meant to silence your anger, but a balm that allows the fire to burn without scorching your tissues.

These herbs do not erase anger; they honor it as Karla McLaren teaches us—anger is a vital messenger, the body’s first responder. Ashwagandha and Chamomile walk alongside that messenger with respect, helping you integrate refusal as the powerful, embodied act it is.

## Ashwagandha: Rooted Resilience for the Coiled Psoas

Ashwagandha’s Latin name, *Withania somnifera*, translates roughly to “sleep-inducing,” but this is a misnomer if you think of it as a sedative. It is an adaptogen, a plant that modulates the body’s stress response. When your psoas muscle locks in response to threat—whether from an external source or the internalized weight of saying no—Ashwagandha supports the nervous system’s capacity to regulate itself.

Imagine the psoas as a tightly coiled spring at the base of your spine, holding your core in a protective embrace. In moments of refusal, this coil tightens, bracing you against emotional or physical threat. Ashwagandha’s earthy bitterness seeps into the nervous system like a slow river, easing the muscle’s grip without asking you to let go of your boundary. It says: You can hold firm and still breathe. You can say no and still find peace.

If you are curious to try this ally, [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a potent addition to your apothecary. Taken as a tincture or a warm decoction, it grounds the nervous system, allowing the psoas to soften its grip while maintaining the strength of your refusal.

## Chamomile: Soothing the Trapezius and the Alarm

While Ashwagandha works deep in the core, Chamomile addresses the surface tension—the tight trapezius, the clenched masseter, the shallow breath. Chamomile is often dismissed as a gentle bedtime tea, but in the realm of somatic herbalism, it is a fierce protector of the nervous system. It contains apigenin, a compound that binds to receptors in the brain to reduce anxiety and initiate muscle relaxation.

When your trapezius is pulled taut, carrying the weight of a refusal you’ve had to hold for too long, Chamomile acts as a release valve. It doesn’t ask you to forget why you are angry; it simply asks your muscles to stop carrying the burden so heavily. A strong infusion of [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) can soften the edges of a clenched jaw and invite the breath deeper into the belly.

Chamomile is the herb of the exhale. It is the medicine you reach for when the refusal has been spoken, and the body needs to remember that it is safe to rest.

## An Embodied Practice: The Breath of Refusal

To integrate the medicine of these herbs and the wisdom of your body’s refusal, try this short embodied practice. You can do this sitting or lying down, perhaps with a cup of Chamomile tea nearby.

1. **Locate the Tension:** Close your eyes and bring your awareness to your masseter (jaw), your trapezius (shoulders), and your psoas (lower belly). Notice which of these areas feels the most contracted. Do not try to change it yet; simply acknowledge it.
2. **The Inhale of Acknowledgment:** Take a slow, deep breath in through your nose. As you inhale, imagine the breath traveling directly to the area of tension. Silently say to yourself, “I feel this refusal. I honor this boundary.”
3. **The Exhale of Softening:** As you exhale through your mouth, consciously invite that specific muscle to soften by just ten percent. You are not asking it to completely let go—you are simply giving it permission to rest slightly while still holding its ground.
4. **The Integration:** Repeat this breath cycle five times. With each exhale, imagine the tension melting slightly, like ice beginning to thaw. If you are working with Ashwagandha, visualize its grounding energy anchoring your psoas. If you are working with Chamomile, visualize its soothing warmth washing over your trapezius and masseter.

This practice is not about erasing the refusal; it is about teaching the body that it can hold a boundary without exhausting itself. It is the somatic embodiment of saying no with grace and power.

## The Legacy of Lilith and the Body’s First No

The story of Lilith is not just an ancient myth; it is a living, breathing legacy that resides in the fascia and muscle of every person who has ever had to fight for their autonomy. When Lilith refused to submit, she didn’t just speak her truth—she embodied it. Her refusal was a somatic event, a complete alignment of her physical form with her inner boundary. This is the legacy you inherit every time your body says no.

Resmaa Menakem, in his profound work on somatic abolitionism and trauma, teaches that trauma and resilience are both passed down through the body. He emphasizes that healing cannot happen solely in the mind; it must be metabolized through the nervous system. The body’s first no—the clench of the masseter, the tightening of the psoas, the bracing of the trapezius—is part of this metabolic process. It is the body’s way of processing a boundary violation and asserting its right to exist safely.

When you honor your body’s refusal, you are participating in a lineage of somatic healing. You are taking the raw, unfiltered energy of anger and transforming it into a protective, grounding force.

## Metabolizing Refusal: The Wisdom of Resmaa Menakem

Menakem’s work reminds us that the body’s responses are not mistakes. The tension you feel when you say no is not a sign that you are doing something wrong; it is a sign that your nervous system is working exactly as it should. However, if that tension is never released, it becomes stuck in the fascia, leading to chronic pain, exhaustion, and a nervous system that is perpetually braced for impact.

To metabolize this refusal, you must learn to complete the stress cycle. This means allowing the body to feel the anger, express the boundary, and then return to a state of safety. Lilith’s legacy is not just about the refusal itself; it is about the freedom that comes after. It is about the ability to say no and then walk away, allowing the psoas to uncoil and the trapezius to drop.

This is where somatic practices and herbal allies like Ashwagandha and Chamomile become essential. They help the body transition from the high-alert state of refusal back into a state of rest and digest. They teach the nervous system that the threat has passed and that it is safe to soften.

## The Sacred Act of Saying No

Saying no is a sacred act. It is the foundation of your autonomy and the guardian of your energy. But it is also a physically demanding act. It requires the body to mobilize its resources, to brace against pushback, and to hold firm in the face of pressure.

When you understand refusal as a somatic event, you begin to treat your body with the reverence it deserves. You stop judging yourself for the tension in your jaw or the tightness in your belly. Instead, you recognize these sensations as the physical manifestation of your boundaries. You learn to tend to them with care—acknowledging the tension in your trapezius, the coil in your psoas, and the clench in your masseter, while also offering your muscles space to breathe and soften when it is safe.

Menakem’s somatic approach offers tools for this process: grounding, breathwork, slow movement, and compassionate attention to the body’s signals. These practices help you shift from bracing and contraction into a state of embodied presence, where refusal is not a burden but a lived, flexible strength. The body’s first no becomes a source of wisdom rather than a source of chronic tension.

## FAQ: Honest Questions About Refusal and the Body

**Q:** I feel constant tension in my shoulders and jaw. Does this mean I’m holding onto anger or refusal?  
**A:** Yes, very likely. Chronic tension in the trapezius and masseter often reflects unresolved anger or boundary-setting that your nervous system is still holding onto. This isn’t a sign of failure, but a signal from your body that it needs attention, compassion, and perhaps tools like somatic practices or supportive herbs to help release the tension safely.

**Q:** How do I know when to hold my refusal firm and when to soften?  
**A:** This is the art and complexity of embodied refusal. Holding firm is necessary when your boundaries are under threat, but softening is equally vital to avoid chronic tension and burnout. Somatic awareness helps you tune into your body’s signals—like the tightening of the psoas or the grinding of your teeth—and learn when it’s safe to relax and when your body needs to brace. Over time, this balance becomes clearer.

**Q:** Can herbs like Ashwagandha and Chamomile replace therapy or somatic work?  
**A:** No, herbs are allies, not substitutes. Ashwagandha and Chamomile support your nervous system and help ease physical tension, but they don’t replace the work of listening deeply to your body’s stories or processing trauma with a skilled practitioner. Think of herbs as part of a holistic toolkit that includes somatic healing, therapy, and compassionate self-care.

**Q:** What if I don’t feel angry or refuse easily—does that mean I don’t carry Lilith’s legacy?  
**A:** Not at all. Lilith’s legacy is not about constant fury; it’s about the capacity to say no when necessary. Sometimes refusal is quiet, subtle, or even expressed through self-care and boundaries that look like gentleness. The body’s first no can manifest in many ways, and somatic awareness helps you discover how it shows up uniquely in you.

**Q:** How do I begin to listen to my body’s refusal if I’ve been taught to ignore or shame my anger?  
**A:** Begin by creating small moments of curiosity and kindness toward your body’s sensations. Notice where you hold tension—the jaw, the shoulders, the belly—and simply observe without trying to fix it. Reading works like *The Body Keeps the Score* can provide insight and validation. Gradually, with patience and support, you can learn to honor your body’s language and let anger serve as a messenger rather than a source of shame.

---

Lilith’s refusal lives in your body’s first no. It is a fierce, embodied refusal to be diminished, erased, or silenced. Through somatic healing, grounded in the wisdom of teachers like Resmaa Menakem, you can carry this legacy with compassion and strength. Your body remembers. Your refusal is sacred. And within it lies the power to reclaim your sovereignty.
